content_blocks.text:
you are here
primitives and references
67
The case of the pilfered references
 It was a dark and stormy night. Tawny strolled into the programmers’ bullpen like she 
owned the place. She knew that all the programmers would still be hard at work, and she 
wanted help. She needed a new method added to the pivotal class that was to be loaded into the 
client’5 new top-secret Java-enabled cell phone. Heap space in the cell phone’5 memory was 
as tight as Tawny’5 top, and everyone knew it. The normally raucous buzz in the bullpen fell to 
silence as Tawny eased her way to the white board. She sketched a quick overview of the new 
method’5 functionality and slowly scanned the room. “Well boys, it’5 crunch time”, she purred. 
“Whoever creates the most memory efficient version of this method is coming with me to the 
client’5 launch party on Maui tomorrow... to help me install the new software.” 
 The next morning Tawny glided into the bullpen wearing her short Aloha dress. 
“Gentlemen”, she smiled, “the plane leaves in a few hours, show me what you’ve got!”. 
Bob went first; as he began to sketch his design on the white board Tawny said, “Let’5 
get to the point Bob, show me how you handled updating the list of contact objects.” 
Bob quickly drew a code fragment on the board:
 Contact [] ca = new Contact[10];
 while ( x < 10 ) { // make 10 contact objects
 ca[x] = new Contact();
 x = x + 1;
 }
 // do complicated Contact list updating stuff with ca
 “Tawny 1 know we’re tight on memory, but your spec said that we had to be able to access 
individual contact information for all ten allowable contacts, this was the best scheme 1 could 
cook up”, said Bob. Kent was next, already imagining coconut cocktails with Tawny, “Bob,” 
he said, “your solution’5 a bit kludgy don’t you think?” Kent smirked, “Take a look at this 
baby”:
 Contact refc;
 while ( x < 10 ) { // make 10 contact objects
 refc = new Contact();
 x = x + 1;
 }
 // do complicated Contact list updating stuff with refc
 “1 saved a bunch of reference variables worth of memory, Bob-0-rino, 50 put away your 
sunscreen”, mocked Kent. “Not 50 fast Kent!”, said Tawny, “you’ve saved a little memory, but 
Bob’5 coming with me.”.
Why did Tawny choose Bob’5 method over Kent’5, when Kent’5 used less memory?
Five-Minute
Mystery
